note_data.title: Theorems Related with Area
keyPoints: Parallelograms on the same base and between the same parallel lines are equal in area
Triangles on the same base and between the same parallel lines are equal in area
Area of a triangle is half the area of a parallelogram on the same base and between the same parallels
If a triangle and a parallelogram are on the same base and between the same parallels, then area of triangle = 1/2 × area of parallelogram
The median of a triangle divides it into two triangles of equal area
A diagonal of a parallelogram divides it into two congruent triangles of equal area
Triangles having equal areas and lying on the same base have equal corresponding altitudes
The ratio of areas of similar triangles is equal to the ratio of squares of their corresponding sides

definitions.definition: A quadrilateral whose opposite sides are parallel is called a parallelogram
Any side of a parallelogram may be called its base and the perpendicular distance between the base and its opposite side is called the height or altitude
A line segment joining a vertex of a triangle to the midpoint of the opposite side is called a median of the triangle
Two triangles are similar if their corresponding angles are equal and corresponding sides are proportional
The area of a plane figure is the measure of the region enclosed by its boundary

importantQuestions.question: State the theorem about triangles on the same base and between same parallel lines.
importantQuestions.answer: Triangles on the same base and between the same parallel lines are equal in area. This means if two triangles have the same base and their third vertices lie on a line parallel to the base, then both triangles have equal areas.

importantQuestions.question: Prove that parallelograms on the same base and between the same parallel lines are equal in area.
importantQuestions.answer: Given: ABCD and ABEF are two parallelograms on the same base AB and between same parallels AB and CF. To Prove: Area of ABCD = Area of ABEF. Proof: Since ABCD is a parallelogram, AD || BC and AB || DC. Since ABEF is a parallelogram, AE || BF and AB || EF. Given that CF is parallel to AB. In parallelogram ABCD: Area = base × height = AB × h (where h is perpendicular distance between AB and CF). In parallelogram ABEF: Area = base × height = AB × h (same height as both lie between same parallels). Therefore, Area of ABCD = Area of ABEF. Hence proved.

commonMistakes: Students often forget that triangles must be between the same parallel lines for equal area theorem
Confusing the relationship between triangle and parallelogram areas - remember triangle area is half
Not drawing proper diagrams showing parallel lines clearly
Forgetting to state 'given' and 'to prove' in theorem proofs
Writing area formulas without specifying base and height clearly
examTips: Chapter 14 typically has 1-2 MCQs and 1 theorem proof question worth 5-8 marks
Always draw neat diagrams with proper labeling - diagrams carry marks
Memorize the exact statement of all theorems as they appear in STBB textbook
Practice drawing parallelograms between parallel lines - appears every year
Learn the relationship: Area of triangle = 1/2 × Area of parallelogram (same base, same parallels)
formulas: Area of triangle = 1/2 × area of parallelogram (on same base and between same parallels)
Ratio of areas of similar triangles = (ratio of corresponding sides)²
note_data.keyDiagram: Draw two parallelograms ABCD and ABEF sharing the same base AB, with vertices C, D, E, F lying on parallel lines. Show the perpendicular height between the parallel lines. This diagram demonstrates that both parallelograms have equal areas and is essential for theorem proofs.
